info.topictitle: Impact of domestic economic issues on the foreign policies of Italy and Germany
info.parenttitle: German and Italian Expansion (1933-1940) - Causes of expansion

markdown:
## The Impact of Domestic Economic Issues on the Foreign Policies of Italy and Germany


![Great Depression Italy](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1f5b9d6b0c5574e17119aa432989131d3dd97b50-1200x690.jpg)

## The Impact of the Great Depression on Italy


<callout type="note">* **The Wall Street crash** has been labeled by some historians as the **“third great catastrophe”** of the 20th Century, alongside the two world wars.
* **Between 1929 and 1932, world trade declined** around 70%, leading to mass unemployment in many countries.
* There was also a **banking crisis**, so there were **no loans available **and industrial activity plunged into even more recession.
* To safeguard the domestic economy, many nations turned to **protectionist** measures. 
  * This means that they** raised tariffs (imports-exports taxes) in order to stimulate internal production and consumption.**
  * But of course this meant even **less international trade and the crisis only got worse**, especially for countries that were dependent on international loans and commerce.</callout>

1. **The Wall Street Crash (1929) hit Italy hard, and rapidly changed Mussolini's attitude towards foreign policy.**
2. During the 1920s, much of **Italy’s economy was sustained in food, fuel and raw materials imports** and exports of some manufactured goods to Britain, France and the US.
3. But now these trading partners were applying protectionism, **Mussolini turned his attention to other markets** like Yugoslavia, Austria, Hungary, Bulgaria and Romania.
4. **These new trading partners made Mussolini more bald in foreign policy, as he didn’t worry about retaliation from the Western countries.**
5. Additionally, he had to face the **immediate domestic impact of the crisis**:
  1. **Unemployment** rising to** over 2 million by 1933** and a **mandatory wage cut **for** industrial workers.**
  2. **Agricultural workers** also **dropped between 20 and 40% during the decade.**
6. The **broken relations with Britain and France** would also foster **closer ties with Germany.**

![Wall Street Crash (1929)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c35e91b4b08f8a69289d4a47848bc6efa1f79f67-1500x966.jpg)

<callout type="self_review">It is a **combination of domestic need and a “nothing to lose” feeling **that explains why** Mussolini works towards an explicitly aggressive foreign policy in the 1930s.**</callout>

## The Impact of the Great Depression on Germany


1. In the case of Germany, **the Great Depression had a very significant role in the rise to power of Hitler.**
2. The **Weimar Republic** had experienced** some economic stability **and even growth between 1924-1929, but this was torn apart when the Depression hit.
3. As we have seen, in the second half of the 1920, under Stresemann, Germany had followed a **“Fulfillment Policy”. This meant abiding by all the clauses of the Treaty of Versailles.**
4. And it also allowed **Germany to be rehabilitated in the arena of international relations**. 
  1. It signed the **Locarno Pact **and in 1926 joined the **League of Nations.**
5. More importantly, it gave **Germany access to international loans**, especially from the US. The most important of these loans was a programme called the** Dawes Plan.**
  1. **But as the Depression started building up in the US, the loans were recalled **and this made Germany’s economy and social structure crumble.
6. In early 1928, **the Nazis **had only 3% of the seats in the Reichstag (German Parliament), but in **mid 1932 they had 32% of the seats and became the largest party.**
  1. **Unemployment grew massively**, and there is a clear correlation between unemployment growing and** vote for the Nazi party also rising.**
  2. With this public support and other strategies, **Hitler was able to put pressure on the Weimar Republic leaders and became chancellor in January 1933.**
  3. Within a few months **Hitler** had established a single party state and **immediate starting rearmament and reorganized the economy to prepare for war.**
  4. His** racial vision and his need for Lebensraum **made him challenge Versailles and all other international order since the get go.

<callout type="note">More **detailed information about Hitler’s rise **will be presented in **Paper 2 - Topic 10: Authoritarian States and in Paper 3 Europe Section 14: Inter-war domestic developments in European States (1918-1939)**</callout>

<callout type="exam_technique">* While you’re studying, take notes of the** similarities and differences **you can find in these two leaders’ foreign policy.
  * For example, **both leaders had an ideology that paired very well with aggressive expansion, but Mussolini was cautious at the beginning, while Hilter started his expansion immediately after he got to power.**
  * Was it because the **conditions** were different? 
  * Or was Nazi **ideology** more dogmatic than Mussolini’s practical approach?
* **Remember that Comparison and Contrast for Paper 2 and 3 essays are only demanded when the command term is Compare and Contrast**
* It is **not needed if the command term is different (“To what extent”, “Discuss”, “Examine”, “Evaluate”, “Assess”, etc.)**</callout>

![Weimar Republic](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1b7350cb2da9094575d3e8a4bbf524e0e8fb34df-1200x1006.png)
